title: AI researchers continue to leave Google for its rivals
slides: Why are AI researchers fleeing Google for rivals?
DeepMind lost three top scientists this quarter alone.
They're not leaving for higher salaries—surprisingly.
Your next AI breakthrough may come from ex-Googlers.
Ask yourself: who's building your AI future?
thread_text:
1. Why the brain drain?

Three top scientists left DeepMind this quarter. The talent exodus is accelerating.

2. Not about money

They say it’s not the salary. So what’s pulling them away from Google’s resources?

3. Freedom to build

Rivals offer faster cycles and fewer guardrails. Researchers want to ship, not debate ethics.

4. The real winners

Your next AI breakthrough may come from ex-Googlers. The lab you trust might be losing its edge.

5. Who builds your AI?

Check the affiliations on the next paper you read. Do you want your future built by defectors or incumbents?
video_script: Top scientists are leaving Google’s AI labs.
It’s not about higher pay this time.
They want freedom to build faster.
Rivals will get the next breakthroughs.
Who do you trust to build your future?

script: Top scientists are leaving Google’s AI labs.
It’s not about higher pay this time.
They want freedom to build faster.
Rivals will get the next breakthroughs.
Who do you trust to build your future?

instagram.caption:
Three top DeepMind scientists walked out this quarter—and a pattern is emerging.

They're not leaving for bigger paychecks. They're leaving because Google moves too slowly on releasing AI tools, while rivals let researchers ship products without endless ethical debates.

If you build with AI tools, the next breakthrough you rely on may come from someone who just quit Google. That means the platform you trust might be falling behind on speed and real-world impact.

Are your creative tools being built by the people who stayed—or by the ones who walked?

carousel_slides.description: Three top scientists left DeepMind this quarter. The talent exodus is accelerating beyond Google's control.
Researchers say salary isn't the reason. What pulls them away from Google's vast resources is bigger.
Rivals offer faster cycles and fewer guardrails. Researchers choose building over endless ethics debates.
Your next AI breakthrough may come from ex-Googlers. The lab you trust is losing its best minds.
Check affiliations on the next AI paper. Decide if you trust defectors or incumbents.
